config.rationale: Next SGLang run should target the highest-probability throughput lever that has not been tested yet: fp8 on top of the known-stable 65,536-context baseline. The current healthy SGLang point is 4395.5 tok/s at 65,536 context with default-style settings, while the only speculative/NEXTN attempt failed to become healthy, so per the rules the right move is to stop spending the next run on MTP and first improve raw engine throughput with a safer lever. I kept tp=1, pp=1, dp=1, max_model_len=65536, mem_fraction_static=0.8, kv_cache_dtype=auto, prefix caching on, fcfs scheduling, and no extra flags to preserve the proven-stable envelope and isolate the effect of fp8. On a single H100 PCIe with a ~9B Qwen-family model, fp8 is the most likely SGLang change to materially raise throughput; a reasonable expectation is roughly +30% to +70% versus the current SGLang baseline, with the exact gain depending on how decode-bound the benchmark mix is. I am not raising max_model_len yet because for optimize_throughput, reserving more KV cache would likely reduce batching headroom and hide the quantization benefit. I am also not enabling NEXTN here because the previous attempt already failed, and the failed command likely mixed auto-managed speculative settings with manual flags; the proper recovery path is to re-establish a stronger non-speculative SGLang point before any cleaner NEXTN retry. Estimated MTP uplift for the user’s requested SGLang command at context length 262,144 (NEXTN, 3 steps) is modest and lower-confidence than at 65,536 because the huge KV reservation reduces active batching headroom: about +5% to +15% throughput versus non-speculative SGLang at the same 262,144 context if it is healthy, with real risk of near-zero gain or regression on short-output or prefill-heavy traffic. Estimated vLLM gain from --language-model-only on this VLM checkpoint is also modest: roughly +0% to +8% throughput at fixed context/concurrency, occasionally up to ~10% if the saved VRAM converts into meaningfully more admitted KV cache or concurrency. So the best next benchmark is SGLang fp8 at the proven-safe 65,536 context, because it offers much higher expected throughput upside and lower operational risk than another immediate MTP attempt.

smoke_tests.json_mode_detail: No content in response (reasoning model may not support JSON mode)

llm_commentary: Experiment `4197902a91de` is a strong SGLang improvement: it raised throughput to 5184.6 tok/s and cut TTFT p95 to 107.6 ms versus the prior SGLang 65k baseline (4395.5 tok/s, 142.4 ms). Against the full history, though, it is still behind the leading vLLM points on all three goals: lower throughput than 5728.7 tok/s, higher latency than 26.9 ms, and therefore not the best balanced point either. It is a useful frontier-expanding result for SGLang, but not a global win.
docker_command: docker run --name bench-sglang-4197902a91de --gpus all --shm-size 16g --network host -v /root/.cache/huggingface:/root/.cache/huggingface -d lmsysorg/sglang:latest python3 -m sglang.launch_server --model-path Qwen/Qwen3.5-9B --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --tp-size 1 --dtype auto --enable-metrics --mem-fraction-static 0.8 --context-length 65536 --quantization fp8 --reasoning-parser qwen3 --tool-call-parser qwen3_coder
